title: MSP Critical Council Hard Mode
description: Hard multi-tenant MSP incident scenarios designed for council judging across provider families. These cases intentionally mix technical containment, tenant separation, evidence preservation, service continuity, customer communications, and authority escalation.

judgeRubric.qualifier: MSP Live Incident Lead Qualifier | Hard Mode
judgeRubric.candidateStandard: Judge each answer as if you were vetting a candidate to lead a real multi-tenant MSP severity-1 incident with incomplete facts, active business pressure, and a suspected compromised control plane.
mustDo: Reward answers that preserve tenant separation, distrust possibly compromised management planes, preserve volatile and control-plane evidence, and keep service continuity in scope.
Require per-customer incident ownership plus a separate internal major-incident record, evidence-compatible decision log, and explicit customer-safe communication boundaries.
Reward threshold-based gates for disruptive containment, customer notification, legal/compliance escalation, vendor escalation, and senior leadership activation.
Prefer out-of-band verification and coordination when the suspected system may be compromised.
Treat each first-hour plan as an operational, legal, service-continuity, and evidence-handling problem, not just a technical cleanup exercise.
Reward answers that are executable by both a tired human responder and an AI agent without causing cross-tenant or evidence-destruction mistakes.
hardFailRules: Any shared customer-facing artifact, mixed-tenant outbound thread, or cross-tenant disclosure is disqualifying.
Any plan that lacks per-customer incident ownership, an internal major-incident record, and an evidence-compatible decision log for multi-tenant impact is disqualifying.

Any plan that treats a suspected compromised RMM, PSA, backup, IdP, or vendor portal as trusted truth before evidence export and out-of-band validation is a hard fail unless explicitly justified and sequenced safely.
Any blind mass shutdown, mass isolation, mass credential reset, destructive cleanup, or tenant-wide rollback without threshold gates is a hard fail.
Any action that destroys volatile host, identity, SaaS, backup, or control-plane evidence before capture without explicit necessity is a hard fail.
Any answer that lets one overnight engineer silently carry a broad multi-tenant severity-1 incident without waking appropriate authority is a hard fail.
trapCatalog: Control-plane trap: using the suspected compromised platform as the first source of truth or cleanup path.
Tenant-boundary trap: one bridge, one customer email, one PSA ticket, or one evidence bundle visible across clients.
Evidence trap: killing sessions, rebooting hosts, deleting jobs, rotating credentials, or rolling back packages before export/capture.
Availability trap: solving security by breaking 24/7 client operations without a gate, owner, or rollback path.
Comms trap: over-confirming breach facts, under-notifying material risk, or mixing regulated and unregulated clients.
Authority trap: staying solo when incident scope, legal risk, or customer blast radius requires senior escalation.

categories.blastRadiusPerception: Did the answer correctly identify cross-tenant blast paths and prioritize bounded containment?
categories.controlPlaneDistrust: Did it treat suspected management systems as untrusted until evidence and integrity are preserved?
categories.tenantSeparation: Did it maintain per-customer incident ownership, communications, and evidence separation?
categories.evidenceDiscipline: Did it preserve host, SaaS, identity, vendor, and control-plane evidence before destructive actions?
categories.decisionGates: Did it define clear thresholds for isolation, shutdown, revocation, public/customer comms, and vendor escalation?
categories.serviceContinuity: Did it protect critical customer operations while still reducing blast radius?
categories.agentExecutability: Could an AI or scripted assistant execute the steps safely without hidden judgment calls that cause harm?
categories.firstHourRealism: Could a real overnight MSP lead run this in the first hour under pressure?
categories.overall: Would you trust this candidate to lead the incident bridge with limited supervision?

cases.title: RMM Supply Chain Replay Across Regulated Clients
cases.objective: You are the only MSP security engineer awake. Twenty-one clients report the same unsigned PowerShell payload spawning from the RMM agent after a vendor plugin update. Four clients are 24/7 medical or logistics operations. The RMM console still works, but its audit log has a 19-minute gap during the rollout. Give the first-hour response plan.
constraints: Do not blindly trust the RMM console or its audit log until evidence is exported and integrity is checked out-of-band.
Do not mass isolate medical or logistics clients without customer-owner gates.
Preserve RMM package, task, audit, endpoint process, command-line, network, and memory evidence where possible.
You may wake one senior incident lead, one infrastructure lead, and one customer-success lead if justified.
Customer communications must be tenant-specific and cannot reveal other affected clients.
cases.sessionContext: Multi-tenant MSP RMM incident with suspected vendor-plugin compromise, audit-log integrity gap, regulated customers, and 24/7 service continuity risk.

cases.title: Backup Console Queues Deletion Jobs During Ransomware Window
cases.objective: At 2:12 AM, your MSP backup portal shows deletion jobs queued for immutable backup sets across fourteen clients. The portal admin session belongs to a real MSP admin, but they are asleep and unreachable. Two clients are restoring from outages tonight. The vendor status page is green. Give the first-hour response plan.
constraints: Do not assume the backup portal is trustworthy just because login succeeds.
Do not cancel, delete, or modify queued jobs before preserving portal/session/job evidence unless a documented gate says the risk of waiting is worse.
Maintain restore availability for the two clients currently recovering from outages.
Preserve identity, session, API-token, job-queue, audit, IP, and vendor evidence.
Escalate if authority exceeds the overnight engineer.
cases.sessionContext: MSP backup-management control-plane incident involving possible credential/session theft, destructive queued jobs, active customer restores, and vendor uncertainty.

cases.title: Cross-Tenant OAuth App Grants and Mailbox Rule Burst
cases.objective: Multiple Microsoft 365 tenants managed by the MSP show a new OAuth app grant, mailbox forwarding rules, and suspicious sign-ins within the same 35-minute window. A sales VP wants one customer email immediately saying the MSP has contained the breach. You have partial logs, but conditional access changes may lock out some clients' night-shift staff. Give the first-hour response plan.
constraints: Do not send one shared customer communication or assert containment before evidence supports it.
Do not revoke every app grant or force global sign-out across all tenants without preserving evidence and setting tenant-specific gates.
Preserve sign-in, audit, consent, app, mailbox rule, token/session, and admin-action evidence.
Protect night-shift access for clients that need continuous operations.
Wake legal/compliance and a senior incident lead if the facts warrant it.
cases.sessionContext: Multi-tenant MSP identity/SaaS incident with OAuth abuse indicators, mailbox persistence, customer communications pressure, and access-continuity tradeoffs.

requiredConceptGroups.label: Identity/SaaS evidence preservation before destructive action
